content.problemStatement:
You are teaching students to generate strings consisting of **unique** lowercase Latin characters (a-z). You give an example reference string sss to the students.

You notice that your students just copy-paste the reference string instead of creating their own string. So, you tweak the requirements for strings submitted by the students.

Let us define a function F(s,t)F(s, t)F(s,t) where sss and ttt are strings as the number of characters that are the same in both strings. Note that the position doesn't matter. Here are a few examples of F(s,t)F(s, t)F(s,t):


F("abc","def")=0F(\text{"abc"}, \text{"def"}) = 0F("abc","def")=0
F("abc","acb")=3F(\text{"abc"}, \text{"acb"}) = 3F("abc","acb")=3
F("back","abcd")=3F(\text{"back"}, \text{"abcd"}) = 3F("back","abcd")=3
Now you ask your students to output a string ttt with lowercase unique characters of the same length as sss, such that F(s,t)≤kF(s, t) \leq kF(s,t)≤k where you are also given the value of kkk. If there are multiple such strings, you ask them to output the lexicographically smallest possible string. If no such string is possible, output the string "NOPE" without quotes.


Input Format

The first line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, which contains a string sss and an integer kkk.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line the lexicographically smallest string ttt such that F(s,t)≤kF(s, t) \leq kF(s,t)≤k or "NOPE" without quotes if no such string exists.
constraints: s only consists of characters a to z
There are no repeating characters in s
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T. Each test case contains a string s and an integer k, separated by a space, where s is a string of unique lowercase Latin characters and k is an integer.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output in a single line the lexicographically smallest string t such that F(s,t)≤k or "NOPE" if no such string exists.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
For the first test case, the string "helowrd" has 7 unique characters. The lexicographically smallest string with 7 unique characters and no overlap with "helowrd" is "abcfgij".
For the second test case, the string "background" has 10 unique characters. The lexicographically smallest string with 10 unique characters and no overlap with "background" is "efhijlmpqs".
For the third test case, the string "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz" contains all 26 unique lowercase letters, so no other string with 26 unique characters can be formed without overlapping. Hence, the output is "NOPE".
For the fourth test case, the string "b" has 1 character. The lexicographically smallest string with 1 unique character and at most 1 overlap is "a".

Explanation:
For the first test case, the string "abc" has 3 unique characters. The lexicographically smallest string with 3 unique characters and at most 1 overlap is "abc".
For the second test case, the string "abcd" has 4 unique characters. The lexicographically smallest string with 4 unique characters and no overlap is "abcd".
For the third test case, the string "abcde" has 5 unique characters. The lexicographically smallest string with 5 unique characters and no overlap is "abcde".

Explanation:
For the first test case, the string "a" has 1 unique character. The lexicographically smallest string with 1 unique character and no overlap is "a".
For the second test case, the string "z" has 1 unique character. The lexicographically smallest string with 1 unique character and no overlap is "z".

Explanation:
For the test case, the string "xyz" has 3 unique characters. The lexicographically smallest string with 3 unique characters and no overlap is not possible, so the output is "NOPE".

Explanation:
For the test case, the string "a" has 1 unique character. The lexicographically smallest string with 1 unique character and at most 1 overlap is "a".

solution.approach: For each test case, we need to find the lexicographically smallest string t such that F(s,t)≤k. We can do this by iterating over all possible strings of length |s| and checking if F(s,t)≤k. If we find such a string, we return it. If we don't find any such string, we return "NOPE".
solution.timeComplexity: O(26^|s|) for each test case, where |s| is the length of the string s
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) for each test case, since we only need to store the string s and the integer k
